attributes.stem:
A 40-year-old woman presents to her General Practitioner complaining of widespread body pain, chronic fatigue and sleep disturbances. She describes the pain as constant and aching, affecting her neck, shoulders, back, and limbs and these symptoms have been worsening over the last five years. The pain intensity varies but is always present to some degree. She has trouble falling asleep and often wakes up feeling unrefreshed. The patient also experiences occasional headaches and difficulty concentrating, which she attributes to her lack of sleep.
She has gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, which is controlled with pantoprazole. She does not smoke or drink alcohol.

Physical examination reveals a woman of average build who appears fatigued. There are no visible signs of inflammation and joint movements are within normal limits. However, she exhibits diffuse tenderness to palpation in various muscle groups, especially around the neck and shoulders. Her vital signs and neurological examination reveal normal results. Routine laboratory tests, including complete blood count, thyroid function tests, and inflammatory markers, are all within normal ranges.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Fibromyalgia (FM) is a prevalent cause of chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain. Common accompanying symptoms include fatigue, memory difficulties, and sleep and mood disturbances. Physical examinations typically reveal no abnormalities, except for diffuse tenderness in muscle groups across various parts of the body. Patients often describe pain dispersed throughout muscles rather than joints.

Fibromyalgia is often classified as a functional illness and can be linked to other functional disorders such as irritable bowel syndrome and tension headaches. Prolonged psychological or physical stress can trigger central sensitisation changes, amplifying the intensity of usually non-painful stimuli.

Rheumatic diseases like rheumatoid arthritis (RA) may manifest with generalised arthralgias, myalgias, and fatigue. However, characteristic RA features, such as multiple joint swelling and elevated inflammatory markers, do not occur in FM. It is worth noting that FM can coexist with RA, and if a patient with RA also exhibits widespread pain and soft-tissue tenderness, FM is a likely diagnosis.

Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR) can mimic FM, causing myalgia and stiffness in the upper and lower extremities. Patients with PMR tend to be older at onset (>55 years of age) and often present more with generalised stiffness than with severe, widespread pain. Inflammatory markers are elevated in PMR.

Patients with fibromyalgia face a high risk of developing comorbid psychiatric disorders, including depression and anxiety. In this case, depression should be considered, given the patient's reduced energy, sleep disturbances, difficulty concentrating, and somatic symptoms. However, she does not report low mood or anhedonia, which are two major features required to diagnose major depressive disorder (MDD) according to the DSM-5 criteria.

Inflammatory myositis, such as polymyositis and dermatomyositis, causes muscle weakness and fatigue but is not typically associated with diffuse pain. Creatine kinase levels are elevated in patients with polymyositis.
learningPoints: Fibromyalgia is characterised by chronic widespread musculoskeletal pain, often accompanied by symptoms such as fatigue, difficulty concentrating, and sleep and mood disturbances.
Physical examinations in FM typically reveal no abnormalities, except for diffuse tenderness in muscle groups and major insertion points.
references: Macfarlane GJ, Kronisch C, Dean LE, Atzeni F, Häuser W, Fluß E, Choy E, Kosek E, Amris K, Branco J, Dincer Fİ. EULAR revised recommendations for the management of fibromyalgia. Annals of the rheumatic diseases. 2017 Feb 1;76(2):318-28. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27377815/
Clauw DJ. Fibromyalgia: a clinical review. Jama. 2014 Apr 16;311(15):1547-55. https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jama/fullarticle/1860480

The Royal Australian College of general Practitioners. Fibromyalgia [Internet]. The Royal Australian College of general Practitioners; [cited 2023 Nov 2]. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2013/october/fibromylagia
